channel.title: Serenity News Briefing
channel.description: Curated news briefings: Iran/ME conflict, AI/ML, fintech, NYC politics. Updated every 12 hours.

item.title: Saudi Arabia Intercepts Six Ballistic Missiles Aimed at Prince Sultan Air Base
item.description: Saudi Arabia's Defence Ministry confirmed it intercepted and destroyed six ballistic missiles heading for Prince Sultan Air Base in the east of the country. The ministry also reported intercepting several drones, including two near the northeastern city of Hafar Al-Batin. The ministry did not specify the source of the projectiles. Prince Sultan Air Base is a key US military facility that has been used in the ongoing US-Israeli campaign against Iran. The intercepts mark a continuation of overnight Iranian strikes on Gulf military infrastructure as Day 12 transitions into Day 13 of the conflict.

item.title: Israel Launches Fresh Wave of Strikes on Beirut, Targeting Hezbollah Infrastructure in Dahieh
item.description: The Israeli military has launched a new wave of strikes on Beirut, targeting Hezbollah infrastructure in Dahieh — the Hezbollah-dominated southern suburb that serves as the group's political and military headquarters. This is an escalation of Israel's Lebanon operations beyond the Litani River line established earlier in Day 12 operations. Israel had previously struck Beirut's central hotel district (March 8) and issued evacuation orders for Tyre and Sidon. The Dahieh strikes mark sustained pressure on Hezbollah's command infrastructure in the Lebanese capital itself. Lebanon's health ministry has reported 486 killed and 1,300+ injured since Hezbollah entered the war on March 5-6. IDF earlier massed troops and tanks near Nabi Sheet in the Bekaa Valley (eastern Lebanon), opening a second front. Over 95,000 people have been displaced in Beirut suburbs alone.

item.title: CENTCOM Confirms 16 Iranian Mine-Laying Ships Destroyed Near Strait of Hormuz
item.description: US Central Command (CENTCOM) confirmed that US forces have "eliminated" 16 Iranian mine-laying ships near the Strait of Hormuz, releasing video of the strikes. The official CENTCOM count of 16 ships supersedes President Trump's earlier Truth Social post claiming "10 inactive mine laying boats" destroyed. The operation follows CBS News reporting that US intelligence believed Iran was preparing to deploy naval mines using small craft carrying 2-3 mines each, and Trump's warning that any mines would result in consequences "at a level never seen before." CENTCOM's confirmation — with video evidence — marks the first officially documented US naval engagement against Iranian mine-laying activity in the current conflict. The Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed to commercial traffic; ~20% of global oil supply and a third of global LNG normally transits the strait. Brent crude oil prices have spiked to near $120/barrel, falling back to ~$88 on ceasefire speculation. Maersk, CMA CGM, and Hapag-Lloyd have suspended Hormuz transits.

item.title: Zelensky Deploys Ukrainian Drone Experts to Gulf Nations to Share Counter-Drone Expertise
item.description: Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky has announced that Ukrainian military drone experts are on their way to Gulf nations to share counter-drone expertise developed during the Russia-Ukraine war. "Ukraine has the greatest experience in the world in countering attack drones, and without our experience it will be very difficult for the Gulf region, the entire Middle East, and partners in Europe and America to build strong protection," Zelensky said. He conditioned the assistance: "Those now seeking Ukraine's help must continue to assist our own defence – first and foremost, our air defence." The deployment comes as Gulf states face sustained Iranian drone attacks. Ukraine has been a leading innovator in low-cost counter-drone systems after four years of defending against Russian Shahed drone barrages — the same Shahed model Iran has deployed at scale in the current conflict. The announcement is significant because Ukrainian counter-drone expertise (including the P1-Sun interceptor, already deployed to Jordan at US request) closes the cost asymmetry: $20–50K intercept vs $2M+ Patriot missiles.

item.title: Macron to Chair Emergency G7 Leaders Meeting Wednesday on War's Global Economic Impact
item.description: French President Emmanuel Macron will chair a G7 leaders summit on Wednesday (March 12) dedicated to the Iran war's impact on the global economy, the BBC reported at 22:06 GMT. The announcement follows a Macron-Carney phone call in which the leaders agreed to "increase coordination among G7 partners." G7 finance ministers met earlier Tuesday but stopped short of ordering IEA strategic reserve releases, instead tasking the IEA to review market conditions first. The leaders summit signals urgency as oil reached ~$120/barrel peak before settling near $88-93, and Hormuz remains effectively closed to normal shipping. The G7 leaders meeting is the highest-level international economic coordination since the war began Feb 28.

item.title: UAE Air Defence Systems Responding to Active Missile Threat — Public Told to Shelter
item.description: The UAE's National Emergency Crisis and Disaster Management Authority confirmed at 21:31 GMT that "air defence systems are currently responding to a missile threat." The public was instructed to remain in a safe location and follow official guidance. The alert comes minutes after Israel's IDF announced an "additional wave" of strikes on Tehran (21:26 GMT), suggesting coordinated Iranian retaliation targeting Gulf neighbours. The UAE had previously intercepted 262 ballistic missiles and 1,475 drones since the war began on 28 February, with 9 drones hitting UAE soil on Day 12 alone.

item.title: IDF Launches 'Additional Wave' of Strikes on Tehran as Day 12 Intensifies
item.description: Israel Defense Forces (IDF) announced a new "additional wave" of strikes on targets in Tehran on Tuesday evening (21:26 GMT), coming after earlier strikes targeting key command centres of the Iranian armed forces. The fresh wave marks continued escalation as Day 12 of Operation Epic Fury enters its overnight phase, with US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth having declared it the "most intense" day of strikes on Iran so far. Iran's internet blackout remains in effect (240+ hours). Tehran residents had earlier described explosions "20 minutes in a row" overnight.

item.title: Iran Police Chief: Would-Be Protesters Will Be Treated as 'the Enemy' — 'Fingers on the Trigger'
item.description: In direct response to Netanyahu's 21:08 GMT call for Iranians to rise up against the establishment, Iran Police Chief Ahmadreza Radan issued a stark warning on state TV: 'If someone comes at the enemy's bidding, we do not regard them as a protester or anything of the sort; we treat them as we would treat the enemy, and we deal with them in the same way we deal with the enemy.' He added: 'All our forces also have their fingers on the trigger, ready to defend the [Revolution].' BBC Persian notes Iran has recently seen nationwide anti-establishment protests (starting late December) that prompted the deadliest crackdown in the Islamic Republic's history — HRANA confirmed at least 7,000 killed. The two statements together (Netanyahu's uprising call + Radan's shoot-to-kill counter) define the high-stakes endgame tension entering Day 13.

item.title: Netanyahu to Iranian People: 'Remove Your Tyrants and Seize the Moment' — Regime Change Explicit
item.description: Israeli PM Netanyahu posted a direct address to the 'people of Iran' on X, explicitly calling for them to overthrow the Ayatollah regime. Key quotes: 'The Ayatollah is no more, and I know you don't want him replaced with another tyrant. So you must act. We are creating the conditions for you to do so.' Netanyahu added: 'In the coming days we will create the conditions for you to grasp your destiny' and 'When the time is right, and that time is fast approaching, we will pass the torch to you.' The post makes explicit what US/Israeli military action is ultimately aimed at — popular uprising to topple the Islamic Republic — and signals imminent escalation toward what Netanyahu frames as an endgame window. The 'in the coming days' language is the most direct regime-change timeline statement from either government.

item.title: Iran Formally Accuses Israel at UN of Deliberately Killing Four Diplomats in Beirut Hotel Strike
item.description: Iran's UN Ambassador Amir Saeid Iravani sent a formal letter to UN Secretary-General Antonio Guterres accusing Israel of the 'deliberate' and 'targeted' killing of four Iranian diplomats at the Ramada Hotel in central Beirut on Sunday. Iran says the diplomats had moved from their official residences to the hotel and were killed while serving as 'official representatives of a sovereign Member State.' The letter calls the strike 'a grave terrorist act and a serious breach of international law.' Israel's IDF countered that the five men killed were Quds Force (IRGC overseas operations) operatives conducting a secret meeting — not protected diplomats. Lebanese health ministry confirmed 4 killed, 10 injured. The formal UN complaint marks an escalation in the legal and diplomatic dimension of the war alongside the ongoing military campaign.

item.title: Trump: US Destroyed 10 Iranian Mine-Laying Boats — 'More to Follow'
item.description: President Trump announced on Truth Social that US forces have struck and completely destroyed 10 Iranian boats attempting to lay naval mines in the Strait of Hormuz, hours after his ultimatum warning Iran of consequences 'at a level never seen before.' Trump declared 'more to follow' and separately edited his earlier Hormuz mines post to add an explicit threat to 'permanently eliminate' any vessel attempting to mine the strait: 'They will be dealt with quickly and violently. BEWARE!' The action escalates the Hormuz confrontation from threat to live military strikes, directly following CBS News reporting that Iran was preparing to deploy mines via small craft carrying 2-3 mines each.

item.title: Canada's Carney: G7 Must Release Strategic Oil Reserves — IEA Meeting Tuesday to Decide
item.description: Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney called on G7 nations to tap their strategic petroleum reserves to counter oil price spikes caused by Iran's Hormuz blockade. Speaking in the House of Commons, Carney said in French: 'The best option is de-escalation, to have peace,' before adding: 'We need to use the G7 oil reserves.' The International Energy Agency (IEA) is meeting on Tuesday to discuss coordinated release of strategic stockpiles. G7 finance ministers met earlier in the day but stopped short of ordering a release — instead directing the IEA to review market conditions and supply security first. The Hormuz closure has driven oil prices to their highest level since Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022. This marks the first formal G7-level multilateral economic coordination triggered by the Iran war. Context: MEMORY notes a potential 300M barrel IEA emergency release was already being coordinated per earlier G7 emergency meeting coverage. Carney's public call adds political pressure ahead of the IEA decision.

item.title: Trump Warns Iran: Hormuz Naval Mines Will Trigger Consequences 'at a Level Never Seen Before' — CBS: Intel Says Iran Preparing to Deploy
item.description: Donald Trump posted directly on Truth Social at 19:54 GMT, warning Iran that placing naval mines in the Strait of Hormuz would trigger military consequences 'at a level never seen before.' His exact words: 'If Iran has put out any mines in the Hormuz Strait, and we have no reports of them doing so, we want them removed, IMMEDIATELY! If for any reason mines were placed, and they are not removed forthwith, the Military consequences to Iran will be at a level never seen before. If, on the other hand, they remove what may have been placed, it will be a giant step in the right direction!' The Truth Social post is tagged as BREAKING by BBC. Critically, Trump's public denial ('we have no reports') is directly contradicted by CBS News (BBC's US partner), which reports US intelligence assets believe Iran IS actively preparing to deploy naval mines using small craft carrying two to three mines each. Hormuz naval mines would represent a severe new escalation — they are difficult to detect and clear quickly, would force complete suspension of commercial traffic, and could trigger direct US Navy mine-sweeping operations. The Trump post functions simultaneously as a public ultimatum, a deterrent signal, and — given the CBS intel report — likely a pre-justification for forthcoming military action against Iranian naval assets or mine-laying craft.

item.title: Russia: Isfahan Consulate Damaged in Strikes — Zakharova Calls It 'Flagrant Violation' of International Law
item.description: Russia's Foreign Ministry confirmed its consulate in Isfahan, Iran was damaged over the weekend when strikes hit the nearby local governor's office. Spokesperson Maria Zakharova called the incident a 'flagrant violation of international law' but did not directly accuse any country of responsibility. No casualties or serious injuries were reported. Zakharova said: 'The conflict in the Middle East continues to escalate, directly or indirectly affecting the interests of an increasing number of states and their civilians.' The incident carries acute diplomatic weight given Russia's contested role in the war — Washington Post reported Russia is providing real-time targeting intelligence on US assets to Iran, a claim Araghchi confirmed on NBC but which Putin denied to Trump in their March 9 phone call. Hitting a Russian diplomatic facility — even collaterally — raises the stakes of Moscow's involvement and gives Zakharova a public grievance platform without escalating to a direct accusation.

item.title: Iran Strikes UAE's Ruwais Refinery — Middle East's Largest, 922K bpd — 9 Drones Penetrate Air Defences
item.description: Iran launched a drone strike on the UAE's Ruwais Industrial Complex on Tuesday evening, home to the biggest refinery in the Middle East. The Abu Dhabi Media Office confirmed a fire broke out at the ADNOC-operated site, with no injuries reported. Reuters and AFP sources said the refinery halted operations as a precaution. ADNOC's Ruwais complex produces 922,000 barrels of oil per day — making this the largest single energy infrastructure target struck in the UAE so far. The UAE Ministry of Defence said 8 ballistic missiles and 26 drones were intercepted on Tuesday, but 9 drones got through air defences. In response to the surge in overnight attacks, the UAE has changed its national early warning system: mobile phone alerts will now be a quiet ping rather than the loud repeated beep used earlier in the conflict, to avoid waking residents unnecessarily during overnight salvos.

item.title: Pentagon: ~140 US Service Members Wounded Since War Began — 8 Remain Severely Injured
item.description: Pentagon spokesperson Sean Parnell released an official casualty statement Tuesday: approximately 140 US service members have been wounded since Operation Epic Fury began on February 28. "The vast majority of these injuries have been minor," Parnell said, with 108 service members already returned to duty. Eight service members remain listed as severely injured and are receiving the highest level of medical care. The US has previously confirmed 8 killed in action — bringing the total combat casualty figure to approximately 148 US military personnel. The first 6 KIA were Army reservists killed in a drone strike at a Kuwaiti port on March 1; the 7th was Sgt. Benjamin Pennington, who died March 8 from injuries sustained at Prince Sultan Air Base; an 8th KIA was confirmed on Day 11.

item.title: Leavitt: War Ends With Iran's "Complete Unconditional Surrender" — Navy Has NOT Escorted Tanker Through Hormuz
item.description: White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt said at Tuesday's briefing that the initial US timeline was four to six weeks to destroy Iran's missiles and navy, eliminate its nuclear capabilities, and wipe out its proxies — but the operation is ahead of schedule. The war will not end until Iran's "complete and unconditional surrender, whether they say it or not," Leavitt said, adding that Trump will personally determine when Iran no longer poses a direct threat. Leavitt also explicitly contradicted US Energy Secretary Chris Wright's now-deleted tweet, clarifying that the US Navy has NOT escorted an oil tanker through the Strait of Hormuz. Wright's post had claimed "the US Navy successfully escorted an oil tanker through the Strait of Hormuz to ensure oil remains flowing to global markets" — the claim briefly sent oil prices from $94 to $82 a barrel before Wright deleted it. Leavitt's denial restores the baseline: Hormuz remains contested, and the Navy is still drawing up "additional options" per Trump's directive.

item.title: White House Briefing: Iran Missiles Down 90%, 50+ Ships Destroyed — Trump "Not Afraid" to Use New Hormuz Options
item.description: White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt delivered the official US battle damage assessment at the daily briefing (18:29-18:33 GMT, Day 12). Key lines: Iran's ballistic missile attacks are down more than 90% and drone attacks down ~85% since Operation Epic Fury began. The US has destroyed more than 50 Iranian naval vessels. President Trump is "committed to allowing the free flow of oil through the Strait of Hormuz," and the US military is drawing up "additional options" following Trump's directive — options Leavitt declined to specify, saying only "the president is not afraid to use them." On energy: Leavitt called the current price surge "temporary," saying Americans will see oil and gas prices "drop rapidly" once military objectives are "fully achieved." She framed the operation as removing "terrorists who indiscriminately target civilians and attempt to hold the global economy hostage." No ceasefire timeline given. The briefing came as Iran fired a fresh missile salvo at Israel (18:02 GMT) and oil had already dropped to $82/barrel earlier in the session on Hormuz escort signals. (Day 12, ~18:33 GMT)

item.title: BREAKING: Iran Launches Fresh Missile Salvo at Israel — IDF Intercepts Active, Alerts Sent Nationwide
item.description: The Israeli Defense Forces report a new wave of Iranian missiles launched at Israel's borders, with interception operations underway. The IDF posted on social media: "Upon receiving an alert, the public is instructed to enter a protected space and remain there until further notice." Mobile phone alerts were sent to affected areas across Israel. The new launch comes on the same day US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth declared it would be the "most intense" day of US strikes on Iran — with "the most fighters, the most bombers, the most strikes." Iran's retaliatory fire continues despite Hegseth claiming Iran had fired the fewest missiles in a 24-hour period since the war began. Meanwhile, White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt's daily briefing is imminent. Oil had dropped to $82/barrel earlier on ceasefire signals — new launches may reverse that trend. (Day 12, BREAKING ~18:02 GMT)

item.title: Oil Drops to $82 as US Energy Secretary's Deleted Tweet Claims Navy Escorted Tanker Through Hormuz
item.description: Brent crude plunged from $94 to $82 per barrel at 17:30 GMT Tuesday after US Energy Secretary Chris Wright posted — and then deleted — a claim that the US Navy had successfully escorted an oil tanker through the Strait of Hormuz. Wright's now-deleted X post read: "President Trump is maintaining stability of global energy during the military operations against Iran. The U.S. Navy successfully escorted an oil tanker through the Strait of Hormuz to ensure oil remains flowing to global markets." The post has since disappeared, but its market impact was immediate and sharp — a ~$12 drop in under an hour. Oil remains well above pre-war levels (~$72/barrel). The deletion raises questions about whether the escort was premature to announce or politically sensitive. BBC Business News editor Mark Broad confirmed the content and timeline. This is the first public statement — even if retracted — of an active US Navy escort mission through the Hormuz blockade zone, a development the market treated as a major ceasefire signal. (Day 12, ~17:49 GMT)

item.title: BREAKING: Fresh Explosions Hit Tehran — BBC Persian and AFP Confirm New Wave of Strikes
item.description: More explosions have been heard in Tehran, with BBC Persian colleagues reporting the Iranian capital is currently being targeted. Journalists from Agence France-Presse (AFP) confirmed hearing three explosions in the city. The BBC live blog headline was simultaneously updated to lead with "Explosions in Tehran" — a BBC editorial signal of a new active strike wave. This comes as US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth had earlier declared today would be "the most intense day" of US strikes on Iran so far, with "the most fighters, the most bombers, the most strikes." The new explosions add to an already intense Day 12 of the conflict.
item.source: BBC News Live / BBC Persian / AFP

item.title: Iranian Red Crescent: 19,000+ Civilian Units Damaged — 77 Medical Centres, 69 Schools Among Casualties
item.description: Iran's Red Crescent Society reports more than 19,000 civilian "units" have been damaged since the war began: 16,000+ residential and 3,000+ commercial. Among specifically damaged facilities: 77 pharmaceutical and medical centres, and 69 schools. Data sourced via US-based Human Rights Activists News Agency (HRANA). The figures emerged as US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth separately said the US would investigate allegations of civilian casualties in Iran, adding: "No nation takes more precautions to ensure there's never targeting of civilians than the United States of America." The Red Crescent damage count represents the most comprehensive tally of civilian infrastructure destruction yet reported for the 12-day conflict.
item.source: BBC News Live / Iranian Red Crescent Society via HRANA

item.title: Three US B-1 Bombers Depart RAF Fairford as UK Denies Involvement in Iran Strikes
item.description: BBC reporter Ellie Price, on the ground at RAF Fairford in south-west England, reports three B-1 bombers took off while she watched. Plane-spotters and photographers at the base counted 12 B-1 bombers total after four more landed earlier in the day. Engines were running on additional aircraft and personnel were carrying out checks. Neither the UK Ministry of Defence nor the Pentagon has confirmed whether any of these aircraft have been involved in bombing missions against Iran. The deployment of US strategic bombers to UK sovereign territory — and their active departure — represents direct observable evidence of the US bombing campaign operating from British soil.

item.title: UK Working on "Range of Options" to Protect Hormuz Shipping — Energy Secretary Calls BP and Shell as HMS Dragon Departs
item.description: Downing Street confirms the UK government is working closely with allies to mitigate threats to UK shipping through the Strait of Hormuz. Energy Secretary Ed Miliband has personally called BP and Shell to address concerns about vessel safety. The Department for Transport is providing guidance to affected companies. Asked whether the UK would deploy militarily to the shipping lane, the PM's spokesman said the government's "priority remains de-escalation and a return to diplomacy" — stopping short of committing to a naval escort mission. HMS Dragon (Type 45 Destroyer) departed Portsmouth earlier today for Cyprus, joining RFA Lyme Bay already at Gibraltar. The UK's Strait of Hormuz posture mirrors France's: Macron announced a "purely defensive" escort mission preparation on Day 12. About 20% of global oil normally transits Hormuz; traffic has fallen ~90% since the war began.

item.title: BREAKING: HMS Dragon Departs Portsmouth for Cyprus — First Royal Navy Major Warship in Mediterranean Since War Began
item.description: HMS Dragon, a Royal Navy Type 45 Destroyer, has departed Portsmouth loaded with ammunition and is heading to Cyprus. The UK had no major Royal Navy warship in the Mediterranean when the war began — HMS Dragon fills that gap. PM Starmer confirmed the deployment a week ago after a drone struck the runway at RAF Akrotiri in Cyprus (described by the MoD as causing "minimal damage"). The ship joins RFA Lyme Bay (a medical/landing ship currently in Gibraltar) as the second UK vessel being deployed to the Eastern Mediterranean. British forces have also shot down drones in Iraqi airspace and logged 230+ flying hours on air defence operations since the war started.

item.title: Oil Falls to $88 as War Optimism Grows — Saudi Aramco CEO Warns of 'Catastrophic Consequences' if Hormuz Stays Blocked
item.description: Trump's comments that the war was "very complete, pretty much" triggered a sharp oil price drop: Brent crude now at ~$88/barrel, down from a Monday peak of ~$119 (-26%). UK natural gas month-ahead delivery fell to 126p per therm, down from yesterday's peak of 171p. Equity markets rallied — FTSE 100 up 1.8%, S&P 500 turned positive at +0.4% after opening flat. Saudi Aramco CEO warned of "catastrophic consequences" if the Strait of Hormuz remains blocked; traffic through the strait has nearly halted across all 12 days of the war. Energy price relief is driven entirely by ceasefire-end optimism — Hormuz blockade remains in effect and any escalation could reverse gains rapidly.

item.title: Iran War Earns Russia €510M/Day — 14% Windfall Above February Average, Boosting Putin's War Economy
item.description: Russia is earning an estimated €510 million per day in fossil fuel export revenues since the US-Israeli strikes on Iran began February 28 — 14% more than its February daily average, according to the Centre for Research on Energy and Clean Air (CREA). BBC Monitoring chief analyst Vitaliy Shevchenko reports the figure is "only likely to rise" as the crisis continues. CREA's Vaibhav Raghunandan: "There's a huge amount of sudden gains over the past week... The longer this crisis continues, the better it is for Russia." He called it "a gut punch for Ukrainian support" and "a huge oxygen boost for Russia." Russia's oil and gas account for about a quarter of its state revenue. The windfall explains why Russia has been providing Iran with real-time US warship/aircraft targeting data (WaPo, March 6) — a war that indefinitely raises global oil prices is worth more to Moscow than any weapons deal.

item.title: UAE Releases War Totals: 262 Missiles + 1,475 Drones Fired at Emirates — 9 Drones Hit UAE Soil Today
item.description: The United Arab Emirates Ministry of Defence has released cumulative war statistics for Operation Epic Fury (Day 12). Total weapons directed at the UAE: 262 ballistic missiles (241 destroyed, 21 evaded or fell in the sea) and 1,475 drones (1,385 intercepted, 90 reaching UAE territory). Today alone: 9 ballistic missiles (8 destroyed, 1 fell in sea) and 35 drones (26 intercepted, 9 fell on UAE soil). Six deaths confirmed — Emirati, Pakistani, Nepalese, and Bangladeshi nationalities. The 9 drones landing in UAE territory today marks a notable increase in penetration rate on Day 12, coinciding with Hegseth's "most intense" US strikes pledge. UAE MoD affirms "high alert and readiness."

item.title: British Airways Cancels All Middle East Flights Through March 28 — Amman, Bahrain, Doha, Dubai, Tel Aviv Grounded
item.description: British Airways has cancelled all flights to Amman, Bahrain, Doha, Dubai, and Tel Aviv up to and including 28 March 2026, as the US-Israel war on Iran continues into Day 12. Abu Dhabi will not restart until October (a winter-only route). BA is still operating two repatriation flights from Oman this week and a reduced service to Larnaca, Cyprus. The cancellations add BA to a growing list of carriers and shipping companies suspending Middle East operations: Emirates grounded all flights through March 31 after Dubai airport was struck; Maersk, CMA CGM, and Hapag-Lloyd have suspended Hormuz transits. The corporate planning horizon now stretches to at least late March, reflecting market expectations that the conflict will not end imminently despite Trump's "pretty quickly" remarks.

item.title: Larijani Threatens Trump Personally: 'Be Careful, So That You Won't Get Eliminated'
item.description: Ali Larijani, Iran's top security official, posted a direct personal threat to US President Donald Trump on X: "Be careful, so that you won't get eliminated." In the same post he said Iran is "not scared of Trump's worthless threats" and that "even those bigger than you couldn't eliminate Iranian nation." The threat is a direct response to Trump's Truth Social warning that Iran would be hit "TWENTY TIMES HARDER" if the Strait of Hormuz is blocked. This is Larijani's second escalatory post of the afternoon — following his six-language Hormuz ultimatum at 14:05 GMT. A direct personal threat to a sitting US president from Iran's top security official is without precedent in this conflict. BBC viewership spiked back to 57,220 on this report. Day 12.

item.title: Iran's Top Security Official Issues Hormuz Ultimatum in 6 Languages: 'Peace and Prosperity' or 'Defeat and Suffering'
item.description: Ali Larijani, Iran's top security official, posted a formal Hormuz ultimatum in six languages (a clear signal it was intended for the international audience): "Strait of Hormuz will either be a Strait of peace and prosperity for all or will be a Strait of defeat and suffering for warmongers." This is the highest-level formal Iranian statement on Hormuz of the war, coming as: oil prices remain ~$90/barrel (down from $120 peak); US Gen. Caine confirmed the military is "looking at options" for naval escorts; France's Macron preparing a formal Hormuz escort mission; Iran's IRGC separately declared no oil would flow from the region; and Iran's FM Araghchi publicly contradicted the IRGC claiming the Strait is "not closed." Larijani's six-language statement bridges civilian and military messaging — framing it as a choice to be made by "warmongers." BBC latest: 14:05 GMT. Day 12.

item.title: Israel Strikes Hezbollah Infrastructure in Dahieh, Beirut — Lebanon Front Escalates on Day 12
item.description: The Israel Defense Forces launched a fresh wave of strikes targeting "Hezbollah infrastructure" in the Dahieh suburb of Beirut — Hezbollah's political and military stronghold in south Beirut. The IDF announced the strikes via Telegram. This marks further escalation on the Lebanon front on Day 12 of Operation Epic Fury, coming after IDF issued evacuation orders for Tyre and Sidon earlier in the day and troops massed near the Bekaa Valley. Iran has already struck Israel, Qatar, and UAE simultaneously in a broadest single salvo of the war earlier on Day 12. BBC latest: 13:51 GMT. Viewership ~58,800.

item.title: UK Scores First Air Defence Kill of War: British Unit Destroys Iranian Drone in Iraqi Airspace Overnight
item.description: The UK Ministry of Defence confirmed a British ground-based counter-uncrewed aerial systems (C-UAS) unit shot down a drone in Iraqi airspace overnight — the first confirmed UK air defence kill of Operation Epic Fury. The drone was "heading towards coalition forces." British pilots have now completed more than 230 flying hours on defence operations across the region. The UK is also readying the RFA Lyme Bay (currently in Gibraltar) for possible deployment to the Eastern Mediterranean, as HMS Dragon (Type 45 destroyer) is also being prepared. Meanwhile on Day 12, BBC latest: 13:44 GMT. Viewership ~58,800. War status: no ceasefire signal.

item.title: IDF Hits IRGC Underground Weapons Research Complex; 30+ Alerts in Northern Israel; Qatar Says It Cannot Mediate While Under Attack
item.description: Three significant developments on Day 12 (13:02-13:29 GMT): First, the Israeli military confirmed it struck an underground complex used by Iran's Revolutionary Guard for weapons research overnight — a new category of precision target, going deeper into Iran's defence industrial base and raising questions about proximity to nuclear research infrastructure. Second, BBC Middle East correspondent Yolande Knell reports that millions of people across northern and central Israel and the occupied West Bank have had to rush for shelter three times today, with northern Israel recording at least 30 warnings of incoming missiles and drones — reflecting sustained Iranian retaliatory pressure despite the 90% missile degradation figures cited by Hegseth. Netanyahu's latest comments say Israel is working to "dismantle Iran's theocratic leadership" but hopes Iranians will "break the yoke of tyranny" themselves — BBC analysis notes this leaves "wriggle room" for a US-Israel exit even without achieving full regime change. Third, at Qatar's foreign ministry briefing, spokesperson Majed al-Ansari said attacks on civilian infrastructure including desalination plants "would lead to humanitarian catastrophe" and called attacks on regional energy facilities a "dangerous precedent." On Hormuz mediation: Qatar said "a viable solution would be to stop attacks against our energy facilities" — rejecting a naval escort as the fix. Critically, al-Ansari confirmed Qatar's communication channels with Iran are "not cut" but said Doha "cannot mediate when it is being attacked by Iran." Qatar's strategic defence partnership with the US "is not in question." BBC correspondent Barbara Plett Usher reported hearing explosions and emergency alerts on her phone as she left the Qatari foreign ministry — Iran was firing at Qatar in real time during the diplomatic briefing.

item.title: Hegseth: Today Will Be 'Most Intense' Day of War; Iran Missiles Down 90%; US Considers Hormuz Escort; Mojtaba Injury Reports Unconfirmed
item.description: US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and Chairman of the Joint Chiefs Gen. Dan Caine held a major Pentagon press conference (12:05-12:39 GMT, Day 12), the most detailed US military briefing since Operation Epic Fury began. Key statements: Hegseth declared today would be "yet again, our most intense day of strikes inside Iran — the most fighters, the most bombers, the most strikes. Intelligence more refined and better than ever." He added Iran fired the "lowest number of missiles since the start of the war" in the past 24 hours. Gen. Caine provided updated degradation figures: ballistic missile attacks down 90% from peak; one-way attack drones down 83%; more than 50 Iranian naval ships struck since Day 1. Caine confirmed targeting of Iran's drone manufacturing infrastructure. On Mojtaba Khamenei: Hegseth declined to confirm or deny reports the new Supreme Leader has been injured, saying only that he "would be wise" to heed Trump's message not to pursue nuclear weapons — the first Pentagon acknowledgment of injury reports. On the Strait of Hormuz: Caine said the US is "looking at a range of options to set the military conditions" to escort vessels through the strait — the first public US confirmation of active Hormuz escort planning, mirroring Macron's announced French mission. Hegseth on war's end: "Our will is endless, but ultimately the president gets to determine the end state." On civilian casualties: "No nation takes more precautions to ensure there's never targeting of civilians than the United States of America" — direct response to BBC Verify's Tomahawk analysis near the Minab school. BBC viewership hit 67,726 during the presser — highest reading of Day 12.

item.title: Iran Launches Simultaneous Missile Strikes on Israel, Qatar and UAE; IDF Confirms New Tehran Wave; Hegseth Pentagon Presser Imminent
item.description: In a significant simultaneous escalation at 11:36 GMT on Day 12, Iran launched fresh missile and drone attacks targeting Israel, Qatar, and the UAE at the same time — the broadest single salvo of the war. The Israel Defense Forces said defensive systems were operating and warnings sent to affected areas. The UAE Ministry of Defence confirmed air defences were "currently responding to incoming missile and drone threats from Iran," instructing residents to adhere to safety instructions. Qatar's Ministry of Defence confirmed armed forces had intercepted a missile attack targeting the country. The triple-target attack comes hours after IDF announced a new daytime wave of strikes on Tehran (10:33 GMT), with AFP journalists in Tehran confirming two explosions in the eastern part of the city (11:24 GMT) — the first independently confirmed daytime blasts since the campaign shifted to overnight-only strikes. US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and Chairman of the Joint Chiefs Gen. Dan Caine are due to speak at the Pentagon at 12:00 GMT — BBC's Tom Bateman expects messaging on American supremacy and market-calming signals around the Strait of Hormuz. Oil prices currently sit around $90/barrel, down from Monday's near-$120 peak, after Trump's comments about ending the war "pretty quickly." The BBC live page has grown to 3 pages and viewership has surged to 48,600+ — the highest reading of the day — consistent with a breaking major escalation event.

item.title: Iran Death Toll Hits 1,332 as Government Reports; Araghchi Says No Realistic US-Israel 'Endgame' and Rejects Negotiations
item.description: Iran's government spokesperson Fatemeh Mohajerani confirmed 1,332 people have been killed since the start of the war on February 28 — up from the Al Jazeera tracker figure of 1,255 reported on Day 11. Among the dead are 206 women and children; Mohajerani said dozens of health centres have been targeted by strikes. The updated toll comes as Iran's internet blackout reaches 240 hours — the most severe government-imposed nationwide shutdown on record globally (NetBlocks). Separately, Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi gave a wide-ranging interview to PBS NewsHour Monday night, issuing several significant diplomatic signals: on negotiations, he said he does not believe talks will be "on the table" as Iran has "very bitter experience of talking with Americans"; on US-Israeli strategy, he stated "I don't think they have any realistic endgame in their mind" and believes they "have failed to achieve all of their goals so far"; on Mojtaba Khamenei's appointment, he called it a "continuation" that provides "some sort of stability"; on the Strait of Hormuz, he confirmed Iran "has not closed" it and "we are not preventing them to navigate in that strait" — directly contradicting the IRGC's "one litre of oil" ultimatum issued hours earlier, underscoring the Mosaic Defence split between civilian and military command. Araghchi also acknowledged "some collateral damage" from Iranian strikes but denied targeting civilian locations. Mojtaba Khamenei has still not appeared publicly or issued any statement since his appointment as Supreme Leader was announced Sunday night.

item.title: IDF Launches New Daytime Wave of Strikes on Tehran; UKMTO Confirms 13 Maritime Incidents in Gulf Including 10 Attacks on Shipping
item.description:
Two simultaneous escalations confirmed on Day 12:
IDF NEW DAYTIME WAVE ON TEHRAN: The Israel Defense Forces announced a new wave of strikes on the Iranian capital Tehran at 10:33 GMT — daytime strikes, a shift from the overnight bombardment pattern. No specific targets confirmed. Tehran residents had already described the previous night as the worst yet: "total darkness," cracks in walls, strikes lasting "20 minutes in a row." The new wave follows IDF ambassador Zarka's statement that Israel is "ahead of schedule" and Mojtaba Khamenei remains "on the list."
UKMTO MARITIME WAR CONFIRMED: The UK Maritime Trade Operations (UKMTO) is investigating a loud bang heard near a bulk cargo ship approximately 36 miles north of Abu Dhabi at approximately 08:00 GMT on Monday. UKMTO reports it has received 13 total incident reports involving vessels in and around the Persian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman since the war began — including 10 confirmed attacks. Three tankers have already been damaged since hostilities began, and one seafarer killed. Major shipping lines Maersk, CMA CGM, and Hapag-Lloyd have all suspended Hormuz transits, rerouting via Cape of Good Hope (adding 10-14 days). Marine insurers have cancelled Gulf war risk cover. The Abu Dhabi incident comes as Iran's IRGC threatens no oil will transit Hormuz and Saudi Aramco's CEO warns of "catastrophic consequences." Day 12, Operation Epic Fury.

item.title: Iran Strikes Haifa Oil Refinery; Hundreds of Thousands Rally for Mojtaba in Tehran; Israeli Death Toll Hits 11; Maronite Priest Killed Refusing Evacuation
item.description:
Day 12 escalation roundup from Al Jazeera and wire services:
IRAN HITS HAIFA: Iran's army launched a drone attack targeting an Israeli oil and gas refinery and fuel tanks in the port city of Haifa — the first confirmed Iranian strike on Israel's northern energy infrastructure. The Israeli army confirmed missiles from Iran were incoming and said interception efforts were underway.
PRO-MOJTABA RALLIES IN TEHRAN: Hundreds of thousands of Iranians rallied in Tehran in support of new Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei — a significant counter-narrative to diaspora protests and domestic anti-succession chanting. Supporters described the rally as defiance against attacking nations. Mojtaba has still not appeared publicly or issued a statement since appointment.
ARAK RESIDENTIAL STRIKE: Five people were killed and several injured when a US-Israeli strike hit a residential building in Arak, western Iran (ISNA). Marks geographic expansion of strikes beyond Tehran/Isfahan corridor.
ISRAELIS DEAD: One man died from shrapnel at a construction site near Tel Aviv's Ben Gurion Airport, raising the Israeli death toll from Iranian attacks to 11. Iran's total: 1,255+ killed, ~10,000 injured.
TRUMP'S TALLY: At the Republican Issues Conference, Trump claimed US forces struck more than 5,000 targets in Iran — destroying the navy and air force, 80-90% of missile launchers, and attacking drone manufacturing facilities. Also confirmed 7th US combat death and said families urged him to "finish the job." Said he was "not happy" with Mojtaba's selection.
MARONITE PRIEST KILLED: Lebanese Maronite Catholic priest Pierre al-Rahi was killed by Israeli tank fire in the Christian village of Qlayaa near the Israel-Lebanon border. He had refused an Israeli forced evacuation order for the town. Lebanon death toll: 486+ killed, 700,000+ displaced.
MACRON HORMUZ MISSION: French President Macron said France and allies are preparing a formal mission to reopen the Strait of Hormuz — first concrete European military commitment to Hormuz security.

item.title: Saudi Aramco CEO Warns of 'Catastrophic Consequences' as Hormuz Crisis Deepens; Pakistan Launches Naval Protection Mission
item.description:
Trump threatens Iran with 'death, fire and fury' over the Strait of Hormuz as the global energy crisis enters its most dangerous phase. Saudi Aramco CEO Amin Nasser — head of the world's largest oil exporter — warned the Iran war is the 'biggest crisis' the region's energy industry has ever faced, saying continued disruption to Strait of Hormuz shipping would have 'catastrophic consequences' for global oil markets. Oil briefly surged past $100/barrel before dipping toward $90 on Trump remarks that the war was 'very complete, pretty much.'
Pakistan has launched Operation Protector of the Seas, a named naval security mission deploying warships to escort shipping lanes and protect Gulf energy supplies, as Islamabad relies heavily on Gulf fuel imports. Pakistan is the first non-Gulf, non-NATO nation to launch a dedicated military escort operation in response to the Hormuz crisis.
Iran's IRGC simultaneously threatened it would not allow 'one litre of oil' through Hormuz while US and Israeli attacks continue — a direct contradiction of civilian Foreign Minister Araghchi, who told PBS the strait has 'not been closed.' The split confirms Mosaic Defence: decentralised IRGC field commanders acting autonomously from the civilian government. Markets rebounded sharply: FTSE +1.7%, DAX +2.4%, Nikkei +2.9%, Kospi +5.4% as investors priced in a near-term end. Day 12, Operation Epic Fury.

item.title: BBC Verify Confirms Three UNESCO World Heritage Sites Damaged in Iran Strikes; Seventh US Soldier Returns Home
item.description:
BBC Verify and BBC Persian have confirmed damage to three UNESCO World Heritage sites in Iran resulting from proximity to US-Israeli strikes.
The 17th-century Chehel Sotoun palace and Persian gardens in Isfahan — a UNESCO World Heritage site — sustained interior damage including broken windows and damaged woodwork. The strike targeted Isfahan's provincial government office, located approximately 110 metres from the palace in the city's historic centre. Two other World Heritage sites are within short distance of the target. Isfahan's deputy for cultural heritage accused the US of deliberately targeting historical landmarks; BBC Verify said it was not possible to determine from the footage what the target was or who carried it out.
Earlier BBC Verify reporting confirmed damage to two other World Heritage sites in Tehran: the Tehran Grand Bazaar and the Golestan Palace. Together, three UNESCO-designated sites have now been damaged across Iran's two largest cities, drawing international attention to the cultural cost of the campaign.
Separately, the body of US Army Sgt Benjamin N Pennington — the seventh American soldier killed in Operation Epic Fury — was returned to the United States at a dignified transfer ceremony at Dover Air Force Base overnight. Sgt Pennington died on March 8 from injuries sustained in the March 1 Iranian strike on Prince Sultan Air Base in Saudi Arabia. Vice President JD Vance and Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth attended the ceremony. An eighth US service member has been confirmed killed separately.
Day 12 of Operation Epic Fury continues. Viewership on BBC's live war blog reached 41,000+ as of mid-morning Tuesday, reflecting high audience engagement amid anticipation of further developments. Ceasefire talks remain unconfirmed despite Iran signalling via Gulf intermediaries that it would consider dialogue if the US scaled back its direct role.

item.title: Iran's New Supreme Leader Absent from Public View; Worst Overnight Strikes on Tehran as Israel Orders Tyre and Sidon Evacuated
item.description:
Day 12 of Operation Epic Fury brought some of the most intense overnight strikes yet on Tehran and the neighbouring Karaj region, as residents described repeated attacks over a prolonged period, a "strange white light" in the sky, and power outages across Tehran and Alborz provinces — damage Tavanir, Iran's national energy company, later confirmed, saying power was restored within two hours.
Iran's newly-appointed Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei has not appeared publicly or issued any statement since his selection Sunday night. Iranian state TV, while broadcasting celebratory coverage, referred to him only as a "veteran of the Ramadan war" without clarifying his whereabouts or physical condition — raising questions about whether he may have been injured in the ongoing strikes. Trump said he was "disappointed" by Khamenei's appointment; Israel previously warned it would "continue to pursue every successor."
Israel's military issued new evacuation orders for Lebanon's coastal cities of Tyre and Sidon, warning that IDF forces would target Hezbollah infrastructure in those areas "in the near future" — an escalation beyond the earlier Litani River line orders. The Lebanese health ministry confirmed 486 killed in Lebanon since Israel's offensive began.
Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi, in an interview with PBS NewsHour, said the US and Israel "don't have any realistic endgame in their mind" and that direct negotiations are "not on the table" due to Iran's "very bitter experience of talking with Americans." He separately denied that Iran has closed the Strait of Hormuz — a direct contradiction of the IRGC's recent "one litre of oil" warning, consistent with the Mosaic Defence split between civilian and military messaging.
Gulf states continue intercepting Iranian missiles and drones: Saudi Arabia shot down one ballistic missile and five drones overnight; Kuwait downed six drones; UAE intercepted multiple threats and confirmed its Erbil consulate was hit by a drone (damage, no casualties); Bahrain sounded sirens following Monday's first civilian death in Manama. Iran's internet blackout remains among the most severe on record globally, with the country now offline for a third of 2026.

item.title: Turkey Positions as Mediator After NATO Shoots Down Second Iranian Missile in Turkish Airspace; Markets Rally
item.description:
Day 12 (March 11, 2026) — Turkey's President Erdogan has positioned himself as a potential mediator in the US-Iran war, warning Tehran directly that violations of Turkish airspace "cannot be justified" after NATO shot down a second Iranian ballistic missile over Turkish territory on Monday. In a phone call with Iran's President Pezeshkian, Erdogan said Turkey is "trying to open the door to diplomacy to help bring the war to an end." In a cabinet meeting, he warned: "Despite our sincere warnings, extremely wrong and provocative steps continue to be taken that will jeopardise Turkey's friendship." Turkey is uniquely positioned: it has NATO's second-largest army, shares a 500-year-stable border with Iran, and maintains deeper regime contacts than any other NATO member — the same combination Erdogan used to mediate in Ukraine and Gaza. BBC reporter Emily Wither, filing from the Turkey-Iran border, confirmed that Erdogan is explicitly "positioning himself as a mediator."

Meanwhile, European markets staged a broad relief rally, following gains in the US and Asia overnight. London's FTSE 100 rose 1.7%, Germany's DAX gained 2.4%, and France's CAC 40 climbed 1.9% — more than compensating for Monday's losses. Bank shares led gains: Barclays, Societe Generale, and Deutsche Bank all rose more than 4%. Oil majors BP and Shell — which had risen on Monday when broader markets fell — reversed course, each slipping about 2%. Brent crude ticked back up to approximately $93/barrel after Trump said "we haven't won enough," having briefly fallen to $83 on his "very complete" remarks before that. UK wholesale gas prices fell more than 10% to ~123p/therm (down from Monday's 171p peak, but far below 2022's 640p crisis peak).

Trump's Monday remarks — summarised by BBC correspondent Anthony Zurcher as "lacking in clarity even when pushed" — covered wide ground: he said the war is "very complete, pretty much" and "very far ahead of schedule," but also that "we haven't won enough." He said the US "still has targets in Iran" but they could be taken out "in one day." He opened the door to acquiring Iranian oil ("certainly people have talked about it"), said the US is "nowhere near" sending troops into Iran, and said he is "not happy" with Mojtaba Khamenei as the new supreme leader without specifying who should replace him. On the Minab school strike (168 killed, 110 children), Trump said "whatever the report shows, I'm willing to live with that." He posted on Truth Social that Iran would face "Death, Fire, and Fury" if it blocks Hormuz — hours after saying the war would end "very soon."

IDF issued a specific new evacuation order at 08:41 GMT for the Lebanese coastal cities of Tyre and Sidon, citing planned strikes on Hezbollah infrastructure — escalating beyond the earlier blanket order for all residents south of the Litani River.

item.title: Iran FM Araghchi: US-Israel Has 'No Realistic Endgame' — and Iran Has Not Closed Hormuz
item.description: Iran’s Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi gave a wide-ranging interview to PBS NewsHour (published Tuesday morning, recorded Monday night) that reveals significant internal contradictions in Iran’s war posture and hardening diplomatic lines. “No realistic endgame”: Araghchi said he doesn’t believe the US and Israel have “any realistic endgame in their mind,” accusing them of having failed to achieve all their stated objectives. This is a notable shift in framing — Iran is now positioning itself as the side with a coherent strategy while the US-Israel coalition is adrift. No negotiations: Asked whether Iran was in contact with US officials about ending the war, Araghchi said he doesn’t believe that will “be on the table,” citing Iran’s “very bitter experience of talking with Americans.” This is a harder line than earlier back-channel signals reported by WSJ (March 10) that suggested Tehran was telling Arab officials it would consider dialogue. Araghchi appears to be closing the public door while private back-channels remain possible. Hormuz contradiction — civilian vs. military messaging: In the interview’s most striking moment, Araghchi directly contradicted the IRGC’s recent statements. While the Revolutionary Guards had threatened that “Tehran will not allow even one litre of oil” to be exported from the region, Araghchi told PBS: “Iran has not closed the Strait of Hormuz and we are not preventing them to navigate in that strait.” This gap between IRGC operational statements and the foreign minister’s public position is consistent with Iran’s Mosaic Defence doctrine, in which the IRGC acts independently of civilian leadership. On oil prices, Araghchi said the rise “is not our fault,” blaming the “attacks and aggression made by Israelis and Americans against us.” Mojtaba succession: Araghchi described Mojtaba Khamenei’s appointment as a “continuation” of his father’s regime that provides “some sort of stability.” The moderate framing is somewhat surprising and may be designed to reassure domestic audiences rather than signal policy change. Civilian casualties: The foreign minister acknowledged “some collateral damage” from Iranian strikes on neighbouring countries, but denied Iran has “ever targeted any civilian location, any civilian purpose, any civilian place.” Context: On Day 12 of Operation Epic Fury, Iran’s foreign minister publicly contradicting his own military’s blockade posture is a significant data point. It either signals a genuine split in Iran’s command structure (consistent with Mosaic Defence), or is a deliberate diplomatic softening intended to create space for eventual talks — while the IRGC maintains hard-line operational pressure. The ceasefire pattern from June 2025 involved Iran publicly holding firm while private channels found an opening.

item.title: Day 12 Escalation: IDF Orders Tyre and Sidon Evacuation; Israel 'Ahead of Schedule'; War's End in Sight?
item.description: Three significant developments on Day 12 signal both escalation in Lebanon and mounting pressure toward an endgame. IDF orders evacuation of Tyre and Sidon (08:41 GMT): The Israel Defense Forces issued a new evacuation order specifically targeting Lebanon’s major coastal cities — Tyre and Sidon — ahead of imminent air strikes. IDF Arabic-language spokesman Avichay Adraee posted the alert on X, saying Israeli forces would be targeting Hezbollah infrastructure in specific areas of the cities. The order goes beyond previous broad “south of the Litani” directives to name specific population centers. Tyre, one of Lebanon’s most historic cities with a population of roughly 100,000, and Sidon, the country’s third-largest city, are now directly in the IDF’s sights. The Lebanese health ministry has confirmed 486 killed since the offensive began. Netanyahu: “Breaking their bones and still active” (08:24 GMT): Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said Israel’s military campaign is “breaking the bones” of the Iranian regime. He said the goal is to free Iranians from the “tyranny” of their current government, while acknowledging “ultimately it is up to them.” The statement signals no imminent Israeli unilateral stand-down. Ambassador Zarka: “Ahead of schedule” and Mojtaba “on the list” (08:35 GMT): Israel’s ambassador to France, Joshua Zarka, told BFMTV that Israeli forces are “ahead of schedule on the war objectives,” and that their prediction of a campaign lasting “a few weeks” remains unchanged. On Iran’s new supreme leader Mojtaba Khamenei, Zarka said: “If he continues to follow the same path of his father, he will also be added to the list of those who must be eliminated.” Defense Minister Israel Katz had already called Mojtaba “an unequivocal target.” Endgame context — June 2025 precedent: For context, the June 2025 Twelve-Day War ceasefire (AP/SRN reporting) came together in a 48-hour whirlwind: US strikes on Iranian nuclear sites triggered a “half-hearted, face-saving” Iranian retaliatory strike, which Qatari mediators used as an opening to broker a deal. The formal ceasefire was announced on day 12 of that war. The current conflict is also now on Day 12. Whether the same sequence unfolds — nuclear strikes, face-saving response, ceasefire — remains unconfirmed. Trump has said the war will end “very soon” but “not this week,” suggesting any ceasefire would likely come later in the week. Lebanon civilian situation: The US Embassy in Beirut has advised American citizens to “strongly consider” leaving Lebanon via Middle East Airlines, or to shelter in place. Beirut and southern Lebanon have been under sustained Israeli bombardment for over a week.

item.title: Day 12: Worst Overnight Strikes Yet on Tehran; Markets Partially Recover; UAE Consulate in Erbil Hit
item.description: Day 12 of Operation Epic Fury opens with the most intense overnight bombardment yet on Iran, even as markets begin recovering from Monday’s historic volatility and diplomatic signals multiply. Overnight strikes — worst yet: US-Israeli attacks on Tehran and nearby Karaj were described by residents as worse than any previous night. BBC Persian reporter Ghoncheh Habibiazad spoke to a woman in her 20s: “There was a strange white light in the sky last night... when they hit, there were some fluctuations.” Power cuts hit Tehran and Alborz provinces after overnight explosions; Iran’s national grid operator Tavanir confirmed “damage to parts of the power network” but said electricity was restored within two hours. Gulf under continued barrage: Saudi Arabia intercepted a ballistic missile and five attack drones overnight. Kuwait’s military downed six drones. Bahrain sounded warning sirens following the death of a 29-year-old woman in Manama — the first Bahraini civilian killed — urging residents to shelter. Iran also struck the UAE consulate in Erbil, northern Iraq, causing damage but no injuries. Markets recovering: After Monday’s “most volatile day of oil trading in world history” (BBC Economics editor Faisal Islam) — when Brent crude swung from $90 pre-market to $120 peak, then crashed to $83 on Trump’s comments before settling at $93 — Asian and European markets are rebounding. Japan’s Nikkei closed up 2.9% on Tuesday, recovering from Monday’s 7% plunge. South Korea’s Kospi gained 5.4%. London’s FTSE 100 opened up 1.3%. UK wholesale gas prices fell more than 10% to ~123p/therm (from Monday’s high of 171p). Brent is still ~$20 above pre-war levels ($73 before February 28). Oil context: G7 finance ministers held emergency talks Monday, with reports of a potential coordinated 300-million-barrel strategic reserve release by the International Energy Agency helping temper the price spike. Trump also said the US would consider lifting some oil sanctions “till this straightens out.” Maersk, CMA CGM, and Hapag-Lloyd remain suspended from Hormuz transits. Lebanon escalation: Israel is ordering the urgent evacuation of all of southern Lebanon, south of the Litani River. IDF spokesman Avichay Adraee posted on X: “We reiterate our urgent appeal for you to evacuate your homes immediately.” Israeli airstrikes are continuing against AQAH (Al-Qard Al-Hassan Association), a Hezbollah financial network. The Lebanese health ministry confirmed 486 killed since Israel began its offensive. US Embassy Beirut: The embassy issued a security alert urging US citizens to “strongly consider” leaving Lebanon on Middle East Airlines flights from Beirut Rafic Hariri airport if it is safe to do so, or to “shelter in place.” IRGC stance: The Revolutionary Guards reiterated that Tehran will not allow “one litre of oil” to be exported from the region if US-Israeli attacks continue (Reuters). IRGC spokesman: “Iran will determine the end of the war.” Trump (Monday recap): NBC interview confirmed Trump left open the possibility of acquiring Iranian oil (“certainly people have talked about it”). He told the New York Post the US is “nowhere near” a decision on ordering troops into Iran. Later press briefing: war will end “very soon” but “not this week.” Truth Social: threatened to hit Iran “TWENTY TIMES HARDER” if Hormuz is disrupted.

item.title: Day 11–12: Trump Says War Ends “Not This Week”, IDF Masses on Lebanon Border, G7 Acts on Energy
item.description:
New signals on March 10–11 complicated the predicted ceasefire timeline. At a press briefing, Trump said the Iran war would end “very soon” but then added a critical qualifier: “not this week” — the first explicit time-bound exclusion he has offered. This marks a departure from his earlier “pretty quickly” and “very complete” language and may signal the war continues through mid-March. Yet the same briefing had Trump claim the operation is a “tremendous success” and reiterate his goal of ensuring Iran cannot develop nuclear weaponry — consistent with a nuclear-facility strike still to come.
BBC Verify forensic video analysis confirmed a US Tomahawk cruise missile struck a military base adjacent to the Minab primary school where 168 students and teachers were killed on Day 1 (Feb 28). This is the first independent technical confirmation of US weapons accountability in the Minab strike; Trump had previously said “I don’t know enough about it.”
Israel-Lebanon front escalating: BBC correspondents report troops and tanks massing near the northern border as “speculation mounts that Israel is planning a full-scale ground invasion to fight Hezbollah.” A separate Israeli special forces raid on the Lebanese village of Nabi Chit killed at least 41 people and injured 40, according to Lebanese health officials. This follows IDF’s helicopter landing near Nabi Sheet (March 8–9, Bekaa Valley front) and the first IDF strikes on central Beirut. If ground forces enter Lebanon in force, the conflict opens a new theatre while the Iran air campaign continues.
G7 finance ministers and the IEA met and said they will take “necessary measures” to stabilise global energy supplies — a signal that strategic petroleum reserve releases are imminent. Brent had crossed $100/barrel before retreating to ~$90. Meanwhile, the UK government opened a charter-flight booking portal for Britons stranded in Dubai and the Gulf following mass flight cancellations.
Human interest: Five Iranian national team footballers were granted Australian humanitarian visas after failing to salute during the national anthem at a match, with one critic branding them “wartime traitors.” Australian Home Affairs Minister Tony Burke approved the visas. A separate BBC analysis notes the war has become “fraught with political risk for Trump” as rising energy prices re-ignite cost-of-living concerns in the US and UK.

item.title: Day 11: Bahrain’s First Civilian Death, IRGC 1-Tonne Warheads, Australia Arms UAE — Trump Says War Ending “Pretty Quickly”
item.description:
The war’s geographic and escalatory scope widened on Day 11 (March 10). A 29-year-old woman was killed and eight injured when a residential building in Bahrain’s capital Manama was struck — the first confirmed civilian death in Bahrain from the conflict. Bahrain’s air defenses intercepted 102 missiles and 173 drones in a single day, which Bahrain Defence Force called “sinful Iranian aggression.” Dubai sirens sounded Tuesday morning; Saudi Arabia shot down 2 drones over its oil-rich eastern region; Kuwait’s National Guard downed 6 drones; Qatar intercepted 17 ballistic missiles and 7 drones, forcing QatarEnergy to halt some LNG production. Bapco (Bahrain Petroleum) declared force majeure after refinery strikes. Brent crude hit $120 before settling ~$90 — 24% above pre-war levels.
IRGC confirmed operational deployment of warheads exceeding one tonne — fulfilling its March 9 escalation declaration and rendering Iron Dome-class interception geometry even less effective. IRGC spokesperson Ali Mohammad Naini: “Iran will determine when the war ends.” Araghchi told NBC Meet the Press (March 8): “There needs to be a permanent end to the war. Unless we get to that, we need to continue fighting for the sake of our people and our security.”
Simultaneously, Trump sent contradictory signals. At the Republican Issues Conference in Miami he called the war “a short excursion” that would end “pretty quickly” — the first time he framed it as near-conclusion. Hours later he posted: “If Iran does anything that stops the flow of Oil within the Strait of Hormuz, they will be hit by the United States of America TWENTY TIMES HARDER than they have been hit thus far.” Trump also announced he would lift some sanctions on certain oil producers to ease global supply pressure, a notable policy shift mid-conflict.
Two new coalition contributors emerged: Australia announced it will send missiles and an RAAF surveillance plane to the UAE (PM Albanese, March 10) — expanding the Gulf defense coalition beyond US/UK/Israel. France confirmed it is preparing a “purely defensive” naval escort mission for ships in the Strait of Hormuz “when the war calms” (Macron, March 9). China, France, Turkey, and Russia all separately contacted Iran pressing for ceasefire — while Putin’s settlement proposals from his Trump call remain undisclosed. Iran’s Al Jazeera feature “The Fourth Successor” detailed Iran’s pre-planned long-war doctrine, suggesting IRGC built resilience for an extended campaign regardless of leadership decapitation.

item.title: Endgame Signals: P5 Presses Iran, IDF Asks How War Ends — Ceasefire Window Opens
item.description:
Day 11–12 (March 10–11) produced the clearest diplomatic convergence of the war, with multiple endgame signals pointing toward a ceasefire window:

P5 + allies all contact Iran: Iranian Deputy FM Kazem Gharibabadi confirmed March 9 that China, Russia, and France have all contacted Tehran urging ceasefire — Iran's stated first condition: "no further aggression." The P5+UK pressure is now convergent and public. Russia is simultaneously providing Iran targeting intel on US positions (confirmed by Araghchi) while calling for diplomacy — a dual-track posture that mirrors June 2025, when Moscow helped broker the ceasefire while maintaining its alignment with Tehran.

Senior IDF officials raise exit ramp questions (Foreign Policy/Ignatius, March 9): "A few senior officials" in Israel are quietly voicing concern about the "open-ended attack on Iran" and suggesting alternatives to Trump's demand for unconditional surrender. Decision still rests with Trump, but this is the first documented fracture in US-Israel unified stance — the same pattern that preceded the June 2025 ceasefire.

Trump-Putin phone call (March 9): 1-hour call; Putin "voiced ideas for quick political/diplomatic settlement." Trump is calling Putin while Russia provides Iran targeting intel — signals a US willingness to accept Russian mediation to end the war. Also discussed: Ukraine and Venezuela oil markets.

WSJ back-channel (March 10): Iran telling Arab officials it would consider restarting dialogue if US scales back its direct military role — first substantive back-channel signal since war began.

Trump signals war may be "short-term": Suggested US has deliberately left Iran's electricity grid intact as leverage; framed war as potentially "short-term" in Florida remarks (March 9). Simultaneously left "Amalek"-framing to Netanyahu while privately signaling flexibility.

Diplomatic context vs. June 2025 playbook: In the first Twelve-Day War, the ceasefire came after: US strikes on nuclear facilities → Iran token strike on Al Udeid → Qatar intercept → Qatar PM calls Tehran after speaking with VP Vance → Trump announces ceasefire on Truth Social (staggered, Iran stops first at hour 0, Israel at hour 12). The same mechanism is now active: Iran hit Al Udeid (Day 11), Qatar summoned Iran's ambassador, Qatar PM has been in contact with VP Vance. War is Day 12. If the pattern holds, ceasefire announcement comes Day 12–13.

item.title: Dubai Airport Suspended, IRGC Defies Pezeshkian: Day 7–9 Civilian Infrastructure Escalation
item.description:
Day 7–9 escalation (March 7–9) produced two major civilian infrastructure shocks and a dramatic Iranian command fracture:

Dubai International Airport struck (March 7): Iranian drone attack struck near DXB terminals — world's busiest airport (~90M passengers/year) suspended all operations. Emirates cancelled all flights through March 31. Passengers sheltered in train tunnels. The Print/Reuters reported DXB had already been damaged by missile debris on Day 1 (March 1); the March 7 drone attack was a more targeted follow-on strike. 1M+ passengers stranded, 12,000+ flights cancelled across the region as airspace collapsed.

Pezeshkian halts Gulf strikes — IRGC immediately defies him (March 7): Iranian President Pezeshkian announced the temporary leadership council had approved a conditional halt: no strikes on neighboring countries unless attacks on Iran originate from their territory. He personally apologized for "fire at will" attacks that had damaged Gulf allies. Hours later, Iran fired 16 ballistic missiles and 121 drones at the UAE alone — the largest single attack of the war. Iranian hardliners erupted: lawmaker Mohammad Manan Raeisi called Pezeshkian's remarks "humiliating." IRGC operating independently of civilian chain-of-command — Mosaic Defence doctrine in practice.

Trump response: Called Pezeshkian's apology Iran's "surrender," threatened escalation. "At some point, I don't think there will be anybody left maybe to say 'We surrender.'" Threatened to expand target sets. Trump simultaneously told Times of Israel that war's end is a "mutual decision" with Netanyahu — first admission the US can't unilaterally stop it.

Gulf state civilian infrastructure pattern (Days 1–9): Bahrain desalination plant struck (first water infrastructure in Gulf); King Fahd Causeway struck; Bapco refinery fire/force majeure; Saudi Aramco Ras Tanura offline (550K bpd); QatarEnergy halted world's largest LNG export facility; Dubai airport suspended twice; UAE stock exchanges closed (first since 1990–91 Gulf War). The IRGC's decentralized command is hitting infrastructure its own civilian leaders said it wouldn't.

War is Day 11 (March 10). Ceasefire unconfirmed. Iran's civilian/military split is the defining strategic story of week two.

item.title: Ukraine Becomes the World's Shahed Czar: Drone Experts + P1-Sun Interceptors Deploy to Jordan; 11 Nations Request Help; America's Expensive Missiles vs Ukraine's Cheap Killers
item.description:
Ukraine deploys drone experts and P1-Sun interceptors to Jordan — Ukrainian President Zelensky confirmed March 9 that Ukraine has dispatched drone specialists and interceptor drones to protect US military bases in Jordan from Iranian Shahed attacks. The US formally requested Ukraine's assistance "last week" and a Ukrainian team deployed "the following day." Zelensky: "We reacted immediately." The deployment follows Zelensky's March 4 meeting with Jordan's King Abdullah II, at which Ukraine expressed support and solidarity. (Kyiv Post, March 9)

11 countries formally request Ukraine's Shahed interception expertise — Zelensky told ABC Australia (March 10) he received formal requests from the US, Europe, and 11 countries neighboring Iran. The requests span every layer: interceptor drones, training, electronic warfare techniques, and operational protocols. Ukraine has spent four years being hit by Russian-deployed Iranian Shahed drones and has become the world's foremost expert in countering them — developing cheap, innovative countermeasures that US and Gulf states now urgently need. (ABC Australia, March 10)

The P1-Sun interceptor: Ukraine's cheap answer to Iran's cheap problem — The P1-Sun is Ukraine's domestically developed counter-drone interceptor, designed specifically to down Shahed-type kamikaze drones at a fraction of the cost of US Patriot or THAAD interceptor missiles. Iran's Shahed-136 costs roughly $20,000–$50,000 to manufacture; the US was burning $2M+ Patriot missiles to intercept them. The P1-Sun closes this cost asymmetry. Iran's drones are "breaching US air defence systems and risk depleting America's expensive interceptor supplies," per ABC Australia — making Ukraine's cheap-kill technology the strategic solution. The P1-Sun combines visual tracking, automated targeting, and a physical intercept mechanism to neutralize Shaheds before they reach targets. (ABC Australia, March 10; Reuters, March 7)

The deep irony — Iran supplied Russia with Shahed drones to hit Ukrainian cities. Ukraine, having survived four years of Shahed swarms, has reverse-engineered the countermeasure. Ukraine's expertise is now the primary tool protecting US bases from those same Iranian drones — in the actual country where Shaheds are manufactured. Iran's most effective asymmetric weapon has created its own nemesis. Zelensky also discussed requests at a Staff meeting: "11 appeals from Iran's neighbors, Europe, and America." Ukraine is now the world's Shahed czar — on the defensive side. (UNN, Kyiv Post, ABC Australia, March 9-10)

Day 10-11 drone picture — Iran has fired 2,000+ drones since Feb 28. US Patriot/THAAD stocks in the region are under pressure after Iran destroyed the Jordan THAAD ($500M), UAE THAAD sites, and Qatar AN/FPS-132 ($1.1B) in the first days. The UAE intercepted 17 ballistic missiles and 6 drones in a single overnight on March 9 alone. Saudi Arabia intercepted drones headed for Shaybah oilfield. Qatar intercepted 63 missiles + 11 drones in one attack. Every Gulf state is now running asymmetric math: Iran's drone cost vs. US interceptor cost. Ukraine is the answer to that equation. (Multiple sources, March 9-10)

item.title: Ceasefire Mechanics Watch: What June 2025 Tells Us About March 2026 Endgame — Qatar's Role, DIA Nuclear Reality, Trump-Netanyahu Dynamic
item.description:
The June 2025 template: how the last ceasefire actually happened — With the March 2026 war on Day 10-11 and diplomatic signals emerging, a detailed reconstruction of the June 2025 ceasefire mechanics reveals the precise playbook both sides are likely running now. The pattern is almost identical: IRGC fires a final volley "in the last moments before the ceasefire" (June 2025: 14 missiles, "delivering a historic and unforgettable lesson to the Zionist enemy"), Trump announces deal on Truth Social in staggered format (Iran stops first, Israel joins at hour 12, "war ended" at hour 24), both sides immediately violate it, then it slowly holds. Understanding this script is essential for reading current diplomatic signals correctly.

Qatar's specific brokerage role — confirmed — In June 2025, it was Qatar's PM Sheikh Mohammed bin Abdulrahman Al Thani who personally persuaded Iran to accept ceasefire — after Iran struck Al Udeid (the US base on Qatari soil). Qatar summoned Iran's ambassador immediately after the attack. Qatar's PM then called the Iranians directly after speaking with VP JD Vance. This is the exact same mechanism now in March 2026: Iran struck Al Udeid again on Day 11, Qatar has already summoned Iran's ambassador and said "all red lines crossed." The diplomatic back-channel from Washington to Tehran runs through Doha. (AFP, June 24, 2025)

DIA nuclear assessment: US strikes delayed Iran's program by months, not destroyed it — The Defense Intelligence Agency's early post-strike assessment in June 2025 found that US strikes on Fordow and Natanz "did not destroy the core components of Tehran's nuclear program and likely only set it back by months." Iran's enriched uranium stockpile was not destroyed. Trump declared "THE NUCLEAR SITES IN IRAN ARE COMPLETELY DESTROYED!" on Truth Social — the DIA said otherwise. The same dynamic is almost certainly playing out in March 2026: Natanz entrances bombed again (IAEA confirmed March 3), Isfahan tunnel complex targeted (holds Iran's ~200kg of 60% enriched uranium). Expect Trump to claim total success regardless of DIA findings — and expect CNN/NYT/Reuters to have the actual assessment shortly after. (CNN, NYT, June 24, 2025)

Trump-Netanyahu ceasefire dynamic — In June 2025, Trump posted on Truth Social: "ISRAEL. DO NOT DROP THOSE BOMBS. IF YOU DO IT IS A MAJOR VIOLATION. BRING YOUR PILOTS HOME, NOW!" — after Israel violated the ceasefire with additional strikes. Netanyahu told Trump he "could not cancel the strike." They compromised: one symbolic strike on a radar north of Tehran, Netanyahu announced he "held off a tougher strike." Trump: "I've got to get Israel to calm down." This negotiation pattern — Trump restrains Israel after announcing ceasefire, Netanyahu pushes back, they agree on one symbolic strike — is the near-certain March 2026 script. (Axios, AFP, June 24, 2025)

Post-ceasefire nuclear diplomacy requirements — After June 2025, France, UK, and Germany jointly warned: "Time is running out. We remain ready to use the leverage of snapback [sanctions reinstatement] if a satisfactory agreement is not reached by summer." In March 2026, E3 + US will again demand IAEA access and enrichment freeze as ceasefire conditions. Iran wants sanctions relief; US/Israel want enrichment halt. Oman and Qatar remain the active mediators for any nuclear deal framework. The post-war negotiation is the real endgame both sides are maneuvering toward. (Reuters, AFP, June 24, 2025)

Iran's public framing after June 2025 ceasefire — Iranian President Pezeshkian declared "the end of the 12-day war" and said Iran was "forced into the military conflict to defend itself." The IRGC's Supreme National Security Council claimed Iran "compelled" Israel to "unilaterally halt its aggression." Netanyahu declared "historic victory." Both sides claimed to win. This mutual face-saving formula — Iran calls it heroic resistance, Israel calls it historic victory, Trump calls it the greatest deal ever — is the template for March 2026. Watch for the identical language from all three capitals simultaneously. (IRNA, IDF, AFP, June 24, 2025)

item.title: Nuclear Reckoning: IAEA Confirms 200kg+ 60%-Enriched Uranium at Isfahan; Israel Strikes Tehran & Isfahan; Iran Bets on Endurance War to Force Washington to Blink
item.description:
IAEA: Iran's near-bomb-grade uranium stockpile likely still at Isfahan — IAEA Director General Rafael Grossi confirmed March 9 that Iran holds over 200kg of 60%-enriched uranium, most of it likely stored in Isfahan's tunnel complex — the only major nuclear facility not badly damaged in June 2025 strikes. If enriched further, that stockpile would provide material for approximately 10 nuclear weapons (IAEA yardstick). Iran has not informed the IAEA about the uranium's status since the current war began, and has not allowed inspectors back into bombed facilities. "What we believe is that Isfahan had until our last inspection" the bulk of the near-bomb-grade stockpile, Grossi told reporters.
